fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we've been getting complaints from a few partner integrations that our URL handling is inconsistent — basically they're building things on top of our parsing output and keep hitting weird edge cases that break their pipelines. The core ask is: we need a proper URL parsing library (Python) that actually follows the living web standard the browser folks use, not just a regex slapped on a string. It should handle the full lifecycle: parse a string (maybe against a base URL), read individual pieces back, mutate those pieces, and re-serialize cleanly. Think of it like what we did for the config validation module — same idea where setting an invalid value should never corrupt the existing state, it just silently rejects. Also needs a lightweight 'would this even parse' probe so callers don't have to do a full parse just to validate. Query string manipulation is a big one too — partners are encoding things wrong constantly, the space/plus encoding thing keeps coming up. The multi-file structure thing is non-negotiable per the platform team's last architecture review. Oh and the I/O adapter layer needs to stay completely separate from the actual parsing logic — we got burned on that coupling before. Should read commands from stdin as JSON and write results to stdout. Reach out if you need the old browser compat reference doc.

One extra detail that came up on output formatting: each result line is `key=value` plus a newline. After a parse_url action, the very first line is always `url=valid` or `url=invalid`. After that, every get/set step just appends its own line in order — gets are `fieldname=value`, sets are `set_fieldname=true/false`, except search and hash which use `set_search=ok` and `set_hash=ok`. For search params, `entries` prints `entries` on its own line and then each `key=value` pair on its own line. `get_all` prints `get_all=N` and then each value on its own line. And the lightweight probe should just output `can_parse=true` or `can_parse=false`.

Also wanted to be very explicit on mutation behavior since that was a big source of confusion: if a set fails, the URL has to stay completely unchanged, no partial update, no half-applied state. The set returns false or the relevant failure indicator, and serializing afterward should give exactly what it gave before the attempt. That applies to host, hostname, protocol, port, username, password, and href assignments. The only exception is search and hash setters, which always succeed and return `ok`. Related to that, if a scheme change makes the current port equal to the new scheme’s default, that port should disappear from serialization — port getter becomes empty string and host getter stops including the port. So changing `http://host:443` to `https` drops the `:443` because 443 is the default port for https. And if somebody tries to switch between incompatible URL shapes, like opaque-path to special, that should be rejected.

For `search_params`, when an action comes in with an `init` string, strip one leading `?` before parsing. Then split on `&`, and split each segment on the first `=`. If a segment has no `=`, treat it as a key with an empty-string value. Those pairs then stick around for whatever later ops show up in the same steps array. On encoding, this needs to follow application/x-www-form-urlencoded rules: spaces become `+`, not `%20`, literal `+` signs become `%2B`, and `&` and `=` get percent-encoded when they appear inside keys or values. Non-ASCII text should be UTF-8 percent-encoded. Important bit here is that `get` and `get_all` return the decoded original string, not the encoded version, while `to_string` returns the encoded serialization.

Last thing, `sort` on search params should be a stable sort by key using Unicode code unit comparison, basically lexicographic by UTF-16 code units. If multiple pairs have the same key, keep their original relative insertion order. Empty keys and empty values don’t get any special treatment, they sort like any other string.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: parse_url(url: str, base: Optional[str] = None, steps: Optional[List[Dict]] = None) -> str

trigger_keywords: what is an opaque path URL
how do you detect a non-hierarchical URL
what does has_opaque_path mean
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A URL has an opaque path when its scheme is not a special scheme and the body is not slash-prefixed (e.g., `a:b`). `has_opaque_path` returns `true` for these. The pathname getter returns the raw opaque body. Resolving a relative reference that contributes only a fragment against an opaque-path base keeps the opaque body intact and appends the fragment. The scheme getter still returns `scheme:`.

trigger_keywords: how should percent signs in paths be handled
what happens to malformed percent escapes
stray percent sign in host vs path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A stray `%` that is not followed by two hex digits is tolerated in path components — it is preserved verbatim in the serialized output (e.g., `/%X%` stays as `/%X%`). However, the same malformed escape inside a host component makes the entire URL invalid and the parse returns invalid. Attempting to set a hostname containing a malformed percent escape must be rejected.

trigger_keywords: what does origin return
how is origin computed
what scheme types have a null origin
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `origin` getter returns `scheme://host:port` (omitting the port if it is the default for that scheme). For `file:` URLs the origin is `null`. For opaque-path URLs or schemes without a meaningful authority the origin is also `null`. The port in the origin is only shown if it differs from the scheme's default port.

trigger_keywords: how does set pathname behave for non-special schemes with double slash
what is the /.// marker in href
non-spec scheme path starting with //
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For non-special schemes, if a path is set that starts with `//`, the serialized href inserts `/.//` as a disambiguating prefix (so `non-spec://p` cannot be confused with an authority). However, the `pathname` getter still returns the path exactly as set (e.g., `//p`). Once a hostname is given to the URL, this disambiguation is no longer needed.

trigger_keywords: what is the set port behavior with mixed input
port with trailing letters
port value that starts with digits then text
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When setting a port, a value that begins with digit characters followed by non-digit characters is accepted: only the leading numeric run is used as the port value. A value that begins with a non-digit character (including `-` for negative numbers) is rejected and the port is left empty. A purely numeric value is accepted directly. The URL remains valid throughout.

trigger_keywords: how should the codebase be structured
is a single file acceptable
what modules should be separate
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must use a multi-file structure. The following concerns must be in distinct modules: URL parsing logic, host/IP parsing (domain, IPv4, IPv6), path normalization and dot-segment resolution, percent-encoding utilities, query parameter collection (URLSearchParams), URL serialization, and the stdin/stdout JSON I/O adapter. The core domain logic must have zero dependency on the I/O adapter.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'parse_url', 'search_params', 'can_parse'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the old browser compat reference doc
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The WHATWG URL Living Standard (https://url.spec.whatwg.org/), which governs all parsing, normalization, host classification, percent-encoding, and serialization behavior implemented in this library

context_pointers.vague_reference: the I/O adapter layer needs to stay completely separate from the actual parsing logic — we got burned on that coupling before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The stdin/stdout JSON command dispatcher (execution adapter) must be a separate module/file from the core URL parser, host parser, path normalizer, percent-encoder, query params, and serializer — matching Architecture Constraint #2 (Strict Separation of Concerns) in start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: lightweight 'would this even parse' probe so callers don't have to do a full parse just to validate
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `can_parse` action in the JSON protocol, corresponding to Feature 5 (Parseability Probe), outputs `can_parse=true` or `can_parse=false` without exposing any URL component

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific guidance on that detail — please check the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the relevant feature and look at the WHATWG URL Living Standard for the normative behavior. When in doubt, the test cases are the ground truth for expected output format.

feature10_set_port.json.description: Set the port of a parsed URL. A purely numeric value is accepted; a negative value is rejected. A value that begins with digits and is then followed by non-digit characters is accepted by taking the leading numeric run as the port, while a value that does not begin with a digit is rejected and leaves the port empty. The URL remains valid throughout.

cases.expected_output:
url=valid
set_port=false
port=
set_port=true
is_valid=true
port=80

feature11_set_path.json.description: Set the path of a parsed URL. The new path is reflected when read back, and re-serialization preserves any query already present. For schemes that are not special, a path that begins with two slashes is disambiguated from an authority by inserting a hidden marker segment in the serialization while the read-back path stays as given; giving the URL a hostname removes that marker. Setting a path keeps the URL internally consistent.

feature12_opaque_path.json.description: Handle URLs whose path is opaque (a scheme directly followed by a non-hierarchical, non-slash-prefixed body). Such a URL reports an opaque path, exposes its scheme and the opaque body as the path, and serializes them back unchanged. Resolving a relative reference that contributes only a fragment against an opaque-path base keeps the opaque body intact and applies the fragment.

feature13_set_query.json.description: Set or clear the query (search) component of a parsed URL. Assigning a query makes it readable back with its leading question mark; assigning an empty string clears the query, and on schemes whose body is opaque, clearing the query exposes the remaining encoded path and re-serializes the URL without the query while keeping any fragment.

cases.expected_output:
url=valid
search=?test
set_search=ok
search=
pathname=space   %20
href=data:space   %20

feature14_set_fragment.json.description: Set the fragment (hash) component of a parsed URL. The assigned fragment is readable back with its leading hash marker.

feature15_set_href.json.description: Replace the entire URL of a parsed object by assigning a new full URL string. On success the object adopts every component of the new string, including normalization such as collapsing an alternative numeric host notation to a canonical dotted IPv4 address, observable through the scheme and the serialized form.

feature16_query_params_build.json.description: Build and mutate an ordered multimap of query parameters and observe it through its count, membership tests, single and multi-value lookups, and serialized form. Appending adds a new pair preserving insertion order and allowing duplicate keys; setting collapses all pairs for a key to a single value (in the key's existing position when present); removing deletes either all pairs for a key or only the pairs matching a key and value. The collection can also be built directly from a query string, where an optional leading question mark is ignored and a key with no value is treated as having an empty value.

cases.expected_output:
size=1
has=true
size=2
get_all=2
value
value2

feature17_query_params_encoding.json.description: Serialize query parameters using application/x-www-form-urlencoded rules while preserving the original decoded values for lookup. A space is encoded as a plus sign; a literal plus is percent-encoded; an ampersand and other reserved characters are percent-encoded; non-ASCII text is UTF-8 percent-encoded. Reading a value back returns its decoded form even though the serialized form is encoded.

feature18_query_params_sort.json.description: Stably sort query parameters by key using a comparison over Unicode code units, preserving the relative order of pairs that share a key. After sorting, the pairs are observable in key order, with equal keys keeping their original insertion order and empty keys or empty values handled like any other.

cases.expected_output:
size=4
entries
a=b
a=a
z=b
z=a

feature1_parse_validity.json.description: Parse a URL string, optionally against a base URL, and report only whether the input is accepted as a valid URL. A syntactically valid absolute URL, or a relative reference resolved against a usable base, is accepted; an empty input, a bare fragment with no base, or a host that violates the host grammar is rejected. The output states validity without exposing any parsed component.

feature2_read_components.json.description: Parse a URL and read back its individual components: the scheme, the optional username and password, the host (with and without the port), the port, the path, and the computed origin, along with flags reporting whether the path is opaque and whether the host section is empty. Each requested component is reported on its own line so the full decomposition of one URL is observable at once.

cases.expected_output:
url=valid
has_opaque_path=false
hostname=d
host=d:2
pathname=/
href=http://:%3A%40c@d:2/
origin=http://d:2

feature3_serialization_normalization.jso.description: Parse a URL and read its canonical serialized form. Serialization normalizes the input: a special scheme and its host are lower-cased, ignored or zero-width characters in a host are removed, dot path segments are resolved, and characters not allowed literally in a path are percent-encoded while already-encoded sequences and permitted characters are preserved. A relative reference resolved against a base is serialized as an absolute URL with its path percent-encoded.

feature4_percent_encoding.json.description: Handle percent signs and percent-encoded sequences during parsing. A stray percent sign that is not part of a valid two-hex-digit escape is tolerated in the path and preserved verbatim in the serialized output, but the same malformed escape inside a host makes the URL invalid. Replacing the whole URL string is subject to the same rule, and an attempt to set a host containing a malformed escape is rejected.

feature5_can_parse.json.description: Probe whether a URL string would parse successfully, optionally against a base URL, without producing any parsed result. An absolute URL or a relative reference resolvable against a valid base reports parseability as true; a relative reference against an unusable base, or a string that is not a URL, reports false.

feature6_host_type.json.description: Parse a URL and report the classification of its host: a registrable domain or text host, an IPv4 address, or an IPv6 address. The classification reflects how the host was interpreted by the parser.

feature7_set_scheme.json.description: Change the scheme of a parsed URL and observe the result. Switching between compatible schemes succeeds and re-serializes the URL under the new scheme; switching a scheme is rejected (reported as failure, leaving the URL unchanged) when the transition is not allowed for the current URL shape. When a scheme change brings the host's default port into effect, that port is dropped from the serialization.

feature8_set_host.json.description: Set the host (or the bare hostname) of a parsed URL. For a URL that has an authority, a syntactically valid replacement succeeds and is reflected when reading the host back; an invalid host value is rejected and leaves the URL unchanged. For a URL whose scheme cannot carry a host, the operation is rejected.

feature9_set_userinfo.json.description: Set or clear the username and password credentials of a parsed URL. Assigning credentials makes them appear in the serialized authority; assigning an empty string clears that credential, and clearing both removes the credentials block (and its separators) from the serialization entirely.
